response.description_long:
Clinicore works the unglamorous seam between clinics and insurers: prior authorization, which the site calls the top administrative burden in specialty care. The failure mode it targets is concrete — staff hours sunk into payer portals and phone calls, patients waiting days for approvals, and denials for documentation that was sitting in the chart all along.

The product is a pipeline from order to decision. Clinicore integrates with Epic, Cerner, and athenahealth; when a clinician places an order that needs authorization, it detects the order, pulls the clinical notes and labs the payer's policy requires, assembles the payer-specific submission, and submits it electronically. A rules engine encodes payer policies and keeps them current, and — a notable honesty about how messy this domain is — a clinical operations team reviews edge cases before anything goes out. Status flows back into the EHR so staff work one queue instead of a dozen portals.

The customers are specialty practices and clinic groups in oncology, cardiology, rheumatology, and imaging, from single-site clinics to groups with 40 locations. The claimed results are specific: authorizations submitted in minutes instead of hours, first-pass approval rates above 90 percent, and staff time on authorizations cut by more than half.

Revenue is straightforward vertical SaaS: per provider per month, with volume pricing for groups. That prices the product against the administrative headcount it displaces rather than against the clinical revenue it unblocks, which is the easier sale to a practice administrator.

The founding team maps to the problem's two halves — a practice administrator who lived the burden and two health-data engineers who could read the chart programmatically. Founded in 2020 in Nashville, TN, the company is now 60 people and remote-friendly. The wedge is classic workflow automation in a corner of healthcare where the incumbent is not software but fax machines, hold music, and portal-hopping — and where the rules engine plus human review hybrid suggests the company knows exactly how far automation can be trusted.
